transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Wyatt. I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been thinking a lot about my colleague who’s leaving soon. It’s been weighing on my mind. I just can’t shake the feeling that things are going to change in a big way once they’re gone. It’s hard to imagine how work will feel without them.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like your colleague's departure is really on your mind and creating a sense of unease about the changes it might bring to your work environment. Can you share more about what specifically feels challenging about their leaving?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s just... I’ve always relied on them for support and collaboration. I feel like their absence will create this void that I’m not sure how to fill. It’s like, I worry that everything will start to fall apart without them around. I guess I’m just feeling a bit lost about how to navigate that change. It’s hard to think about what comes next.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like this colleague has been a significant source of support for you, and their absence feels like it will leave a gap that you're unsure how to manage. Feeling lost in the face of change is completely understandable. What emotions are coming up for you when you think about navigating this transition?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’ve been feeling pretty sad and lonely, to be honest. It’s like this heaviness that I can’t quite shake off. I find myself thinking about how things might not be the same, and that makes me feel down. I guess there’s also a bit of trepidation about how I’ll cope with the change. I keep trying to connect with their work, like maybe that will help, but it’s just... it’s tough.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're experiencing a mix of sadness and loneliness, along with some anxiety about how you'll cope with the changes ahead. That heaviness can be really difficult to carry. It’s interesting that you’re trying to connect with their work as a way to cope. Can you tell me more about what that looks like for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, I’ve been looking over some of the projects we worked on together, trying to remember the discussions we had and the ideas we shared. I think I’m hoping that by revisiting that work, I can keep some of their influence alive in my own approach. But it feels a bit futile, like I’m just grasping at something that’s slipping away. I guess I’m trying to hold on to the good parts, but it’s hard to see how I’ll manage without that collaboration.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It seems like revisiting those projects and discussions is your way of trying to preserve the positive aspects of your collaboration, which makes a lot of sense. It’s understandable to want to hold on to those good memories, especially when facing the uncertainty of their absence. I hear you feeling that it might be a bit futile, though, which sounds really frustrating. What do you think it is about that collaboration that you’ll miss the most?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s the sense of connection and understanding we had. We could bounce ideas off each other, and there was this rhythm to our work that made everything feel more manageable. I’ll miss that partnership, the way we could tackle challenges together. It’s like, without that, I’m left feeling a bit isolated. I worry that I won’t have that same level of support or creativity on my own. It just feels daunting to think about facing those challenges without them by my side.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like the connection and understanding you shared with your colleague created a supportive and collaborative environment that made challenges feel more manageable. Losing that partnership is understandably daunting and can contribute to feelings of isolation. It’s clear that their presence brought a lot of value to your work experience. How do you think you might navigate that sense of isolation as you move forward?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’m not really sure, to be honest. I guess I’m hoping that maybe I can find new ways to connect with others at work, but it feels a bit overwhelming right now. I think I might need to reach out more, try to engage with different colleagues, but there’s a part of me that feels hesitant. It’s like, I don’t want to burden anyone with my feelings, but at the same time, I know I can’t just go through this alone. It’s a bit of a tug-of-war in my mind.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're caught in a bit of a tug-of-war between wanting to reach out for support and feeling hesitant about burdening others with your feelings. That’s a tough place to be in, especially when you recognize the need for connection. It’s completely normal to feel overwhelmed by the idea of engaging with new colleagues. What do you think might help you feel more comfortable reaching out to others during this transition?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe starting small could help. Like, just having casual conversations or checking in with a few people I feel a bit more comfortable with. It might make it easier to open up without feeling like I’m diving straight into heavy topics. I guess I just need to remind myself that it’s okay to share what I’m going through, even if it feels a bit vulnerable. But there’s still that nagging fear of being a burden, you know? It’s hard to shake that off.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Starting small sounds like a thoughtful approach, and it’s great that you’re considering casual conversations as a way to ease into connecting with others. It’s completely understandable to feel vulnerable about sharing your feelings, especially with that nagging fear of being a burden. Acknowledging that fear is an important step. What do you think might help you manage that fear as you begin to reach out?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think reminding myself that everyone goes through tough times might help. It’s normal to feel overwhelmed sometimes, and I’m sure others can relate. Maybe I could also focus on the idea that sharing my feelings could actually strengthen connections rather than push people away. It’s just a matter of finding the right moment and the right people to talk to. I guess I’ll have to be patient with myself as I navigate this. It’s just... easier said than done, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you have some insightful thoughts about the potential for shared experiences to strengthen connections. Recognizing that it’s normal for everyone to face tough times can be a comforting perspective. It’s true that finding the right moment and the right people can take time, and being patient with yourself in this process is important. It’s completely valid to feel that it’s easier said than done. What do you think might help you practice that patience with yourself as you navigate these feelings and connections?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think giving myself permission to feel what I’m feeling would be a good start. It’s okay to be sad or anxious about this change, and I shouldn’t rush myself to feel better right away. Maybe I could set small, achievable goals for myself, like reaching out to one person a week, and celebrate those little steps. It might help to keep a journal too, just to process my thoughts and emotions without judgment. I guess it’s about being gentle with myself during this transition, even if it’s hard to remember that sometimes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Giving yourself permission to feel your emotions and recognizing that it’s okay to take your time is a wonderful approach. Setting small, achievable goals and celebrating those steps can create a sense of progress and accomplishment, which is really important during a transition like this. Keeping a journal to process your thoughts and emotions without judgment sounds like a great way to reflect and be gentle with yourself. It’s clear you’re thinking thoughtfully about how to navigate this change. How do you feel about these ideas as you consider moving forward?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit more hopeful, actually. Just talking through these ideas makes it seem more manageable. I like the thought of taking it step by step and giving myself space to feel what I need to feel. It’s reassuring to know that I don’t have to have everything figured out right away. I guess it’s about finding my own rhythm in this transition, and that feels a little less daunting now. Thank you for helping me sort through all of this.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that you’re feeling more hopeful and that talking through these ideas has made things feel more manageable. Finding your own rhythm in this transition is a valuable perspective, and it’s great that you’re allowing yourself the space to feel what you need to. Remember, it’s perfectly okay to take things step by step. If you ever want to revisit any of these thoughts or explore new ones, I’m here to support you. Is there anything else on your mind today that you’d like to discuss?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’ve shared a lot already, and it feels good to have talked it through. I just want to focus on taking those small steps and see how it goes. I appreciate your support and understanding. If anything else comes up, I’ll definitely reach out. Thank you for listening.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Wyatt. I’m glad you found it helpful to talk things through, and I admire your commitment to taking those small steps. Remember, it’s perfectly okay to reach out whenever you feel the need. Take care of yourself, and I wish you the best as you navigate this transition. Have a good day!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I appreciate your kind words and support. I’ll do my best to take care of myself and keep moving forward. Have a great day too!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You're very welcome! I'm glad to hear that. Take care, and remember to be gentle with yourself as you move forward. Have a wonderful day!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I will keep that in mind. I hope you have a wonderful day as well!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Thank you! I appreciate that. Take care, and I wish you all the best on your journey. Have a great day!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you so much! I really appreciate it. I’ll take care, and I wish you all the best too. Have a great day!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You're very welcome! I'm glad to have been here to support you. Take care, and I hope you have a wonderful day ahead!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! It means a lot to have your support. I’ll take care, and I hope you have a wonderful day too!
